domain_summary: Next.js React Server Components represent a server-first architecture using the React Flight protocol to stream serialized component trees, achieving 32-68% bundle size reductions and significant performance improvements. The App Router implements hierarchical file-based routing with parallel data fetching, granular error boundaries, and nested layouts for managing complex applications. React Flight uses a row-based, line-delimited, JSON-like serialization format with '$' prefix reference notation, but remains completely undocumented by the React team—all detailed protocol knowledge derives from CVE-2025-55182 security research. Despite architectural benefits, no official controlled benchmarks with specific metrics exist, and concrete 100+ route implementation examples remain absent from public sources.

claims.claim: Next.js App Router uses file-based routing where the directory structure mirrors the URL structure
claims.notes: Confirmed across multiple outputs including new architecture research

claims.claim: React Server Components are the default in Next.js App Router for better performance and security
claims.notes: Consistently reinforced across outputs but conflicts with Pages Router performance claims

claims.claim: Server Components should be used for data fetching, accessing APIs, and protecting sensitive operations from the client
claims.notes: Reinforced by new data fetching patterns documentation

claims.claim: Client Components should be used for interactive features requiring hooks (useState, useEffect) and event listeners
Enterprise audit of 32 apps migrated to Server Components showed 68% smaller client bundles, 55% faster Time to Interactive (TTI), and zero client-side data exposure
claims.notes: Conflicts with lack of official controlled benchmarks

claims.claim: Case study of e-commerce site using Next.js Server Components showed 67% Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) improvement and 51% bundle size reduction
React Server Components significantly increase page load time and reduce total JavaScript bundle size by eliminating hydration and avoiding JS shipping to clients
claims.notes: Architectural benefit consistently documented

claims.claim: Streaming sends HTML chunks progressively to the browser as they become ready, rather than waiting for the entire page to be rendered on the server before sending
claims.notes: Confirmed in new streaming research

claims.claim: Reverse proxies like Nginx buffer responses by default, requiring proxy_buffering off or X-Accel-Buffering: no header to enable streaming benefits; otherwise chunked stream arrives as one blob
claims.notes: Critical infrastructure issue confirmed in error handling research

claims.claim: React Flight uses a row-based JSON format (not standard JSON) that supports references, React elements, and extended data types like Date, Set, and Map
claims.notes: Superseded by more detailed claim_016

claims.claim: Flight protocol uses a reference system with '$' prefix notation to create cross-chunk references; chunks can reference each other using this syntax
claims.notes: Extensively confirmed through CVE research across multiple outputs

claims.claim: Flight serialization includes a reference resolution mechanism via the 'reviveModel' function in ReactFlightReplyServer.js that maps chunk IDs to deserialized objects
claims.notes: Confirmed through security vulnerability analysis with detailed technical documentation

claims.claim: Pages Router handles 6x more concurrent requests than App Router in heavy server-side rendering scenarios
claims.notes: Methodology unclear; conflicts with established App Router benefits

claims.claim: 70% TTFB reduction claimed for Server Components approach
claims.notes: Unusually high figure not corroborated elsewhere

claims.claim: React Flight is a row-based, line-delimited, JSON-like (but not standard JSON) streaming protocol designed specifically for serializing React component trees and async boundaries, not HTML
claims.notes: Consistently confirmed across multiple technical analysis outputs

claims.claim: Known type tag markers include: $ (basic reference), $@ (chunk reference/promise), $# (async boundary), $B (Blob), $D (Date), $L (lazy), $F (FormData), $S (Set), $W (WeakMap), but React's official documentation does not provide exhaustive enumeration
claims.notes: Identified through security research; completeness unknown

claims.claim: Each Flight message consists of three parts: an ID, a type indicator, and a data payload, followed by a newline character, creating line-delimited rows

React does not publish official technical specifications or RFC documents for the Flight protocol; all detailed protocol documentation has been reverse-engineered from source code commits, security analyses, and CVE-2025-55182 research
claims.notes: Critical finding confirmed across multiple outputs - extraordinary documentation gap

claims.claim: Next.js recommends parallel data fetching as the primary strategy to prevent waterfalls in App Router applications, where requests at the same hierarchical level are initiated simultaneously rather than sequentially

The lowest revalidate value across each layout and page of a single route determines the revalidation frequency of the entire route, ensuring child pages are revalidated as frequently as their parent layouts require
Error boundaries in Next.js App Router must be Client Components ('use client'); they wrap route segments and nested children in React Error Boundaries, catching errors thrown during rendering
claims.notes: Confirmed across error handling outputs

claims.claim: Skeleton loading fallbacks with Suspense improve perceived performance by maintaining layout stability; this strategy prevents cumulative layout shift and creates content-shaped loading indicators instead of blank screens
No official Vercel or Meta published controlled benchmarks comparing pure Server Components vs pure Client Components with specific TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond measurements exist
claims.notes: Critical gap confirmed; articles exist but metrics inaccessible

claims.claim: Large-scale Next.js applications use route groups, nested layouts, parallel routes, and intercepting routes as primary organizational patterns to manage complexity in 100+ route projects

React Flight chunk boundary determination algorithm is COMPLETELY UNDOCUMENTED by the React team—no official specification, RFC, source code comments, or published algorithm exists for precise fragmentation rules, buffer size thresholds, or backpressure mechanisms
claims.notes: Critical documentation gap for production technology

claims.claim: React Flight protocol has NO officially documented error handling specification for streaming failures, chunk corruption, or deserialization errors
claims.notes: All error handling knowledge comes from CVE security research

claims.claim: Concrete directory structure examples for 100+ route Next.js applications do NOT exist in publicly available sources; while architectural patterns are documented, no production-grade example with verified 100+ routes has been published
claims.notes: Most detailed template has only 8-10 routes despite production-scale claims

contradictions.description: App Router performance benefits vs Pages Router handling 6x more concurrent requests
contradictions.resolution: App Router benefits are well-documented for general use cases; Pages Router claim may be specific edge case with unclear methodology

contradictions.description: No official controlled benchmarks exist vs enterprise audit claims of 68% bundle reduction
contradictions.resolution: Enterprise claims lack transparent methodology; official benchmarks remain unavailable despite Server Components being stable

knowledge_gaps.gap: Complete enumeration of ALL type tags supported by Flight protocol—while ~8-10 tags identified through security research, no comprehensive list exists and hidden type tags may remain undiscovered

Exact chunk boundary determination algorithm—precise rules for fragmentation, buffer size thresholds, async await backpressure mechanisms, and how streaming adapts to network conditions or promise resolution delays

Specific FCP numerical benchmarks (milliseconds) comparing pure Server Components vs pure Client Components from controlled experiments by DeveloperWay and Nadia Makarevich—articles exist but numerical results inaccessible via search

React Flight streaming error handling specification—no official documentation exists for how Flight protocol recovers from corrupted chunks, missing references, or deserialization failures during streaming
Detailed directory structure examples for 100+ route applications—architectural patterns documented but concrete implementation examples for e-commerce or SaaS dashboards remain sparse
Quantitative guidelines for optimal Suspense boundary granularity (e.g., how many boundaries before overhead increases) in large streaming applications (100+ routes)

Complete source code examination of packages/react-server/src/ReactFlight.js and ReactFlightServer.js—type tag definitions, serialization algorithms, and chunk flushing logic are not publicly accessible through standard web search
